name: SQLite
what_it_does: A self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration SQL database engine embedded directly into applications. The database is a single cross-platform file. Available in Python's standard library (sqlite3 module), and via better-sqlite3 for Node.js. The most widely deployed database engine in the world, found in every browser, OS, and mobile device.
use_cases: Local agent state persistence without requiring a database server
Prototyping and development before migrating to a server database
Single-user or single-agent applications with moderate data volumes
Read-heavy analytical queries on local datasets (especially with WAL mode)
Embedding structured storage in CLI tools, scripts, and agent frameworks
not_for: Multi-agent concurrent write workloads (write serialization bottleneck)
Distributed or replicated databases across multiple nodes
Applications requiring user-level access control or remote connections
best_when: An agent runs locally or in a single process and needs reliable SQL storage without the overhead of a database server. Ideal for agentic tools, local state management, and development workflows.
avoid_when: Multiple agents or processes need to write concurrently to the same database, or the database needs to be accessed over a network.

auth.notes: No built-in authentication — access is controlled entirely by OS filesystem permissions on the database file. SQLCipher adds AES-256 encryption at rest. For agents, this means the database is as secure as the filesystem it lives on. No network exposure by default, which is actually a security advantage for local workloads. SQLite SEE (Encryption Extension) is available for commercial use.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free, public domain — no license restrictions whatsoever.
pricing.paid_tiers: SQLite Encryption Extension (SEE) from Hwaci: ~$2000 one-time. Litestream (replication) is open source. Turso (distributed SQLite) has free tier.
pricing.notes: SQLite is public domain — no copyright, no license requirements. The most permissive licensing of any major database. Turso (libSQL fork) provides distributed/cloud SQLite with a generous free tier.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits. Write throughput limited by WAL mode and disk I/O (~10,000-100,000 writes/sec depending on hardware). Reads are extremely fast from memory/page cache.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SQLite error messages are functional but minimal — 'UNIQUE constraint failed: table.column' and 'database is locked' are common. Error codes are numeric (SQLITE_BUSY, SQLITE_CONSTRAINT) which are well documented. Python's sqlite3 module raises standard exceptions (IntegrityError, OperationalError) that are intuitive. better-sqlite3 provides synchronous errors that are easier to handle than async databases.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No built-in idempotency. Agents use INSERT OR IGNORE, INSERT OR REPLACE, or UPSERT (ON CONFLICT) syntax. Transactions are synchronous in better-sqlite3 making idempotent patterns easier to implement. Python sqlite3 uses autocommit by default in Python 3.12+.
known_agent_gotchas: SQLITE_BUSY errors occur when multiple processes/threads try to write simultaneously — implement exponential backoff retry logic
WAL mode (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL) is almost always better for agent workloads — enable it explicitly, it is not the default
Python sqlite3 module check_same_thread=True by default — set to False for multi-threaded agents but ensure proper locking
Integer overflow: SQLite uses dynamic typing — Python ints > 2^63 are silently stored as REAL (float) losing precision
The MCP server exposes full read/write access to the file — scope it carefully, consider read-only connections for read-only agents
No concurrent writers: even with WAL mode, only one writer at a time — design agent workflows accordingly
Turso/libSQL extends SQLite with remote access but changes some behaviors — test compatibility if migrating

score_rationale: SQLite excels for local, single-agent workflows — zero setup, zero dependencies, Python stdlib inclusion, and an excellent official MCP server make it highly agent-accessible. The af_score is boosted by the MCP server quality and zero-friction setup. fq_score is limited by the single-writer constraint, absence of network access, user-level auth, and practical size limits. For local agent state and tooling, SQLite is often the best choice. For production multi-agent systems, it is a bottleneck.
